tlframe 0.1.0.9000
  • Get Started
    • ─── Foundations

    • ─── Advanced

    • ─── Internals

Study-Level Automation & Deep Features

Source: vignettes/advanced-features.Rmd
advanced-features.Rmd

This guide covers automation and advanced features. For core pipeline verbs, see vignette("tlframe").

The central question here is: “I can make one table. How do I make 50 efficiently?”

The Three-Tier Defaults System

tlframe resolves settings from four tiers, lowest to highest priority:

+----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| 4. Per-table verbs         fr_page(font_size = 10)          <- wins  |
| 3. Session theme           fr_theme(font_size = 9)                   |
| 2. Project config          _tlframe.yml                              |
| 1. Package defaults        inst/defaults/_tlframe.yml       <- lowest|
+----------------------------------------------------------------------+

Every fr_table() call merges all four tiers. You configure the broadest settings at the lowest tier and override only what changes.

Mental model:

Tier Scope Who sets it When it changes
Package defaults All users tlframe maintainer Never (ships with package)
_tlframe.yml One study/project Lead programmer Once per study
fr_theme() One R session Programmer Per program if needed
Per-table verbs One table Programmer Per table

SAS parallel: Tier 2 is your autoexec.sas + PROC TEMPLATE. Tier 3 is OPTIONS at the top of your program. Tier 4 is inline STYLE()= overrides.

YAML Configuration: Company House Style

Place a _tlframe.yml at your project root. Every table in the project inherits these settings without any R code.

Full file reference 

# _tlframe.yml — place at project root
page:
  paper: letter                       # letter | a4 | legal
  orientation: landscape              # landscape | portrait
  margins: [1.0, 0.75, 1.0, 0.75]    # top, right, bottom, left (inches)
  font_family: "Courier New"          # Any system font
  font_size: 9                        # Points
  continuation: "(continued)"         # Appended to titles on page 2+
  col_gap: 4                          # Inter-column gap in points

columns:
  split: false                        # true = auto-split wide tables

header:
  align: ~                            # null = inherit from column
  valign: bottom                      # top | middle | bottom
  bold: false
  span_gap: true                      # Gap between adjacent spans
  n_format: "{name}\n(N={n})"         # N-count label template

pagehead:
  left: "Protocol TFRM-2024-001"
  center: ~
  right: "CONFIDENTIAL"
  font_size: ~                        # null = inherit page font_size
  bold: false

pagefoot:
  left: "{program}"
  center: ~
  right: "Page {thepage} of {total_pages}"
  font_size: ~
  bold: false

rules:
  hlines: header                      # header | open | booktabs | box | void
  vlines: void                        # box | all | inner | void

footnotes:
  separator: false                    # true = horizontal line above footnotes
  font_size: ~
  placement: every                    # every | last (last = PDF only)

spacing:
  titles_after: 1                     # Blank lines after titles
  footnotes_before: 1                 # Blank lines before footnotes
  pagehead_after: 0
  pagefoot_before: 0
  page_by_after: 1

titles:
  align: center                       # left | center | right
  bold: false
  font_size: ~

tokens:
  study: "TFRM-2024-001"
  compound: "Zomerane"
  cutoff: "15MAR2025"

Loading config 

# Auto-discover: searches up from getwd() for _tlframe.yml
# Falls back to package defaults if not found
fr_config()

# Explicit path
# fr_config("path/to/_tlframe.yml")

# Inspect current config
cfg <- fr_config_get()
cfg$page$font_size
#> [1] 9

# Reset to package defaults
fr_config_reset()

Config-driven tokens

Custom tokens defined in YAML are available in fr_pagehead() and fr_pagefoot():

tokens:
  study: "TFRM-2024-001"
  cutoff: "15MAR2025"
fr_config("_tlframe.yml")

# Now {study} and {cutoff} resolve automatically
tbl_demog |>
  fr_table() |>
  fr_pagehead(left = "Study {study}", right = "Cutoff: {cutoff}") |>
  fr_render("output.rtf")

No R code needed to define the token values — they come from the YAML file.

Theme System: Study-Level Overrides

fr_theme() sets session-level defaults that override config and are overridden by per-table verbs. It follows the ggplot2::theme_update() pattern: call it once at the top of your program.

Setting a theme 

fr_theme(
  font_size   = 9,
  font_family = "Courier New",
  orientation = "landscape",
  hlines      = "header",
  pagehead    = list(left = "TFRM-2024-001", right = "CONFIDENTIAL"),
  pagefoot    = list(left = "{program}",
                     right = "Page {thepage} of {total_pages}"),
  tokens      = list(study = "TFRM-2024-001"),
  spacing     = list(titles_after = 1, footnotes_before = 1)
)

# Verify
theme <- fr_theme_get()
theme$font_size
#> [1] 9
theme$pagehead$right
#> [1] "CONFIDENTIAL"

Every table inherits the theme 

# Both tables inherit the theme automatically — no repeated configuration
spec1 <- tbl_demog |>
  fr_table() |>
  fr_titles("Table 14.1.1", "Demographics")

spec2 <- tbl_disp |>
  fr_table() |>
  fr_titles("Table 14.1.4", "Subject Disposition")

fr_get_page(spec1)$font_size
#> [1] 9
fr_get_page(spec2)$font_size
#> [1] 9

Per-table override 

# This table needs portrait orientation — override just that
spec3 <- tbl_ae_soc |>
  fr_table() |>
  fr_page(orientation = "portrait") |>
  fr_titles("Table 14.3.1", "AE by SOC/PT")

fr_get_page(spec3)$orientation
#> [1] "portrait"
fr_get_page(spec3)$font_size  # Still 9 from theme
#> [1] 9

Reset 

fr_theme_reset()
# Back to package defaults
is.null(fr_theme_get()$font_size)
#> [1] TRUE

Theme vs config

Feature _tlframe.yml (config) fr_theme()
Storage File on disk R session memory
Scope All programs in project Current R session
Set by fr_config() fr_theme()
Priority Lower (tier 2) Higher (tier 3)
Survives restart Yes (file persists) No (session-only)
Best for Company/study-wide standards Program-specific overrides

Recipes: Reusable Pipeline Fragments

Recipes capture fr_*() verb chains as portable objects. Unlike themes, recipes can include column definitions, styles, and spans — anything you can pipe.

Creating recipes 

# Company-wide page layout
company_layout <- fr_recipe(
  fr_page(orientation = "landscape", font_size = 9),
  fr_pagehead(left = "TFRM-2024-001", right = "CONFIDENTIAL"),
  fr_pagefoot(left = "{program}",
              right = "Page {thepage} of {total_pages}"),
  fr_hlines("header")
)
company_layout
#> 
#> ── fr_recipe (4 verbs)
#> 1. fr_page(orientation="landscape", font_size=9)
#> 2. fr_pagehead(left="TFRM-2024-001", right="CONFIDENTIAL")
#> 3. fr_pagefoot(left="{program}", right="Page {thepage} of {tot...)
#> 4. fr_hlines("header")

# AE-specific formatting
ae_style <- fr_recipe(
  fr_header(bold = TRUE, align = "center"),
  fr_page(continuation = "(continued)")
)
ae_style
#> 
#> ── fr_recipe (2 verbs)
#> 1. fr_header(bold=TRUE, align="center")
#> 2. fr_page(continuation="(continued)")

Applying recipes 

spec <- tbl_demog |>
  fr_table() |>
  fr_apply(company_layout) |>
  fr_titles("Table 14.1.1", "Demographics")

fr_get_page(spec)$font_size
#> [1] 9

Composing recipes

c() merges recipes. Later recipes override earlier ones for verbs that replace; verbs that append (fr_spans, fr_styles) accumulate.

# Combine company layout + AE-specific overrides
ae_combined <- c(company_layout, ae_style)

spec <- tbl_ae_soc |>
  fr_table() |>
  fr_apply(ae_combined) |>
  fr_titles("Table 14.3.1", "AE by SOC/PT")

fr_get_page(spec)$continuation
#> [1] "(continued)"

The Three-Layer Recipe Pattern

For a study with 50+ tables, structure recipes in three layers:

# Layer 1: Company standard (shared across all studies)
company <- fr_recipe(
  fr_page(orientation = "landscape", font_family = "Courier New",
          font_size = 9),
  fr_pagehead(right = "CONFIDENTIAL"),
  fr_pagefoot(left = "{program}",
              right = "Page {thepage} of {total_pages}"),
  fr_hlines("header")
)

# Layer 2: Study-specific (shared across all tables in one study)
study <- fr_recipe(
  fr_pagehead(left = "Protocol TFRM-2024-001"),
  fr_page(tokens = list(study = "TFRM-2024-001",
                         cutoff = "15MAR2025"))
)

# Layer 3: Table-type recipes
demog_recipe <- fr_recipe(
  fr_header(bold = TRUE, align = "center",
            n = c(placebo = 45, zom_50mg = 45,
                  zom_100mg = 45, total = 135),
            format = "{name}\n(N={n})"),
  fr_footnotes("Percentages based on N per treatment arm.",
               .separator = FALSE)
)

ae_recipe <- fr_recipe(
  fr_header(bold = TRUE, align = "center",
            n = c(placebo = 45, zom_50mg = 45,
                  zom_100mg = 45, total = 135),
            format = "{name}\n(N={n})"),
  fr_page(continuation = "(continued)"),
  fr_footnotes("MedDRA version 26.0.",
               "Subjects counted once per SOC and PT.",
               .separator = FALSE)
)

# Usage: compose layers + add per-table details
tbl_demog |>
  fr_table() |>
  fr_apply(c(company, study, demog_recipe)) |>
  fr_titles("Table 14.1.1", "Demographics") |>
  fr_cols(group = fr_col(visible = FALSE)) |>
  fr_render("output/Table_14_1_1.rtf")

tbl_ae_soc |>
  fr_table() |>
  fr_apply(c(company, study, ae_recipe)) |>
  fr_titles("Table 14.3.1", "AE by SOC/PT") |>
  fr_cols(soc = fr_col(visible = FALSE),
          row_type = fr_col(visible = FALSE)) |>
  fr_rows(group_by = "soc", indent_by = "pt") |>
  fr_render("output/Table_14_3_1.rtf")

Saving and loading recipes

Recipes are serializable — they store R call expressions, not closures:

saveRDS(company, "company_style.rds")
company <- readRDS("company_style.rds")

This means recipes can be shared across team members via a shared drive or internal R package.

Study Automation Blueprint

For a typical CSR with 50–100 TLFs, combine a metadata table with recipes and a batch loop.

Step 1: Create a metadata CSV 

metadata <- data.frame(
  table_id  = c("14.1.1", "14.1.4", "14.3.1", "14.2.1"),
  title1    = c("Demographics", "Subject Disposition",
                "AE by SOC/PT", "Time to Study Withdrawal"),
  title2    = c("ITT Population", "All Randomized",
                "Safety Population", "ITT Population"),
  data_name = c("tbl_demog", "tbl_disp", "tbl_ae_soc", "tbl_tte"),
  recipe    = c("demog", "demog", "ae", "tte"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
metadata
#>   table_id                   title1            title2  data_name recipe
#> 1   14.1.1             Demographics    ITT Population  tbl_demog  demog
#> 2   14.1.4      Subject Disposition    All Randomized   tbl_disp  demog
#> 3   14.3.1             AE by SOC/PT Safety Population tbl_ae_soc     ae
#> 4   14.2.1 Time to Study Withdrawal    ITT Population    tbl_tte    tte

Step 2: Define recipe library 

recipes <- list(
  demog = fr_recipe(
    fr_header(bold = TRUE, align = "center",
              n = c(placebo = 45, zom_50mg = 45,
                    zom_100mg = 45, total = 135)),
    fr_footnotes("Percentages based on N per arm.", .separator = FALSE)
  ),
  ae = fr_recipe(
    fr_header(bold = TRUE, align = "center",
              n = c(placebo = 45, zom_50mg = 45,
                    zom_100mg = 45, total = 135)),
    fr_page(continuation = "(continued)"),
    fr_rows(group_by = "soc", indent_by = "pt"),
    fr_cols(soc = fr_col(visible = FALSE),
            row_type = fr_col(visible = FALSE)),
    fr_footnotes("MedDRA v26.0.", .separator = FALSE)
  ),
  tte = fr_recipe(
    fr_header(bold = TRUE, align = "center",
              n = c(zom_50mg = 45, zom_100mg = 45, placebo = 45)),
    fr_rows(group_by = "section", blank_after = "section"),
    fr_cols(section = fr_col(visible = FALSE)),
    fr_footnotes("[a] Kaplan-Meier estimate.", .separator = FALSE)
  )
)

Step 3: Batch rendering loop 

company_study <- c(company, study)  # From the three-layer pattern above

for (i in seq_len(nrow(metadata))) {
  row <- metadata[i, ]

  # Look up the pre-summarized dataset by name
  data <- get(row$data_name)

  # Look up the table-type recipe
  recipe <- recipes[[row$recipe]]

  # Build and render
  data |>
    fr_table() |>
    fr_apply(c(company_study, recipe)) |>
    fr_titles(
      paste("Table", row$table_id),
      row$title1,
      row$title2
    ) |>
    fr_render(sprintf("output/Table_%s.rtf",
                      gsub("\\.", "_", row$table_id)))
}

The entire 4-table study output is generated with a 6-line loop. Scale to 50 tables by adding rows to the metadata CSV.

SAS parallel: This replaces the SAS macro library + %include pattern. The metadata CSV is your macro call dataset, recipes are your macros, and the loop is your %DO block.

Column Splitting for Wide Tables

When a table has more columns than fit on a page, fr_cols(.split = TRUE) automatically splits into panels:

spec <- tbl_vs[tbl_vs$timepoint == "Week 24" &
                tbl_vs$param == "Systolic BP (mmHg)", ] |>
  fr_table() |>
  fr_titles("Table 14.3.6", "Systolic BP --- Column Split Demo") |>
  fr_cols(
    param     = fr_col(visible = FALSE),
    timepoint = fr_col(visible = FALSE),
    statistic = fr_col("Statistic", width = 1.2, stub = TRUE),
    .split = TRUE, .width = "fit",
    placebo_base      = fr_col("Placebo\nBaseline", width = 1.0),
    placebo_value     = fr_col("Placebo\nValue", width = 1.0),
    placebo_chg       = fr_col("Placebo\nCFB", width = 1.0),
    zom_50mg_base     = fr_col("Zom 50mg\nBaseline", width = 1.0),
    zom_50mg_value    = fr_col("Zom 50mg\nValue", width = 1.0),
    zom_50mg_chg      = fr_col("Zom 50mg\nCFB", width = 1.0),
    zom_100mg_base    = fr_col("Zom 100mg\nBaseline", width = 1.0),
    zom_100mg_value   = fr_col("Zom 100mg\nValue", width = 1.0),
    zom_100mg_chg     = fr_col("Zom 100mg\nCFB", width = 1.0)
  ) |>
  fr_hlines("header")

Key concepts:

  • Stub columns (stub = TRUE) repeat in every panel. Use for row labels.
  • .split = TRUE enables automatic panel splitting when total width exceeds the printable area.
  • .width = "fit" scales each panel to fill the page width.
  • Panel widths are calculated proportionally — wider columns get more space.

Complex Styling Patterns

Conditional styling with fr_style_if

fr_style_if() applies styles based on data values at render time. Unlike fr_rows_matches() which selects rows, fr_style_if() evaluates a condition per cell:

spec <- tbl_demog |>
  fr_table() |>
  fr_cols(group = fr_col(visible = FALSE)) |>
  fr_styles(
    # Bold all group header rows
    fr_row_style(
      rows = fr_rows_matches("group", pattern = "^[A-Z]"),
      bold = TRUE
    ),
    # Tint the total column
    fr_col_style(cols = "total", bg = "#F0F4F8")
  )
#> ℹ `fr_rows_matches()`: no rows matched in column "group".
#> ℹ Selector: pattern ^[A-Z].

Style precedence

When multiple styles target the same cell, the last one wins. Order your fr_styles() calls from broadest to most specific:

spec <- tbl_demog |>
  fr_table() |>
  fr_cols(group = fr_col(visible = FALSE)) |>
  fr_styles(
    # 1. Base: tint total column (broadest)
    fr_col_style(cols = "total", bg = "#F0F4F8"),
    # 2. Bold group headers
    fr_row_style(
      rows = fr_rows_matches("group", pattern = "^[A-Z]"),
      bold = TRUE
    ),
    # 3. Specific cell override (most specific — wins)
    fr_style(rows = 1, cols = "total", bg = "#003366", fg = "#FFFFFF")
  )
#> ℹ `fr_rows_matches()`: no rows matched in column "group".
#> ℹ Selector: pattern ^[A-Z].

Zebra striping 

spec <- tbl_disp |>
  fr_table() |>
  fr_styles(
    fr_row_style(rows = seq(2, nrow(tbl_disp), 2), bg = "#F5F5F5")
  ) |>
  fr_hlines("header")

Header region styling 

spec <- tbl_demog |>
  fr_table() |>
  fr_styles(
    fr_style(region = "header", bold = TRUE,
             bg = "#003366", fg = "#FFFFFF", align = "center")
  ) |>
  fr_hlines("header")

RTF vs PDF: What Differs

The same spec produces both formats, but some features behave differently:

Feature RTF PDF
Rendering engine Direct RTF 1.9.1 control words tabularray + XeLaTeX
Footnote placement "last" Not supported (repeats every page) Works (lastfoot)
Page numbering \chpgn / \field codes \thepage / \pageref
Continuation text Plain text on page 2+ LaTeX \fancyhead
Decimal alignment \ql\li<offset> (left + indent) \hspace{pt} + ~
Fonts Referenced by name (viewer uses local) Embedded via fontspec
Multi-line cells \line (RTF line break) \newline (LaTeX)
Group pagination R-side pagination (with group_by) longtblr + \trkeep

Font handling on Linux:

RTF files reference fonts by name — the viewing application uses whatever fonts are installed locally. PDF files embed fonts via XeLaTeX’s fontspec. On bare Linux systems without Microsoft fonts, tlframe automatically falls back to bundled Liberation fonts:

System font Liberation fallback
Courier New Liberation Mono
Arial Liberation Sans
Times New Roman Liberation Serif

PDF requirements

PDF rendering requires XeLaTeX. Install via tinytex:

install.packages("tinytex")
tinytex::install_tinytex()

Or use your system’s TeX distribution. tlframe compiles twice for correct page references.

QC Patterns

Pre-render validation 

spec <- tbl_demog |>
  fr_table() |>
  fr_titles("Table 14.1.1", "Demographics") |>
  fr_cols(group = fr_col(visible = FALSE))

result <- fr_validate(spec)
result$valid
#> NULL

fr_validate() checks:

  • Column names match the data frame
  • group_by, page_by, indent_by reference valid columns
  • Style row/column indices are in range
  • Widths are positive and reasonable
  • Span columns exist and are contiguous

Use strict = TRUE in CI pipelines to fail on warnings.

Accessor-based QC checks

The fr_get_*() functions enable programmatic quality checks:

spec <- tbl_demog |>
  fr_table() |>
  fr_titles("Table 14.1.1", "Demographics") |>
  fr_footnotes("Source: ADSL.", .separator = FALSE) |>
  fr_cols(group = fr_col(visible = FALSE))

# Check title count
stopifnot(length(fr_get_titles(spec)) == 2)

# Check footnote content
fn <- fr_get_footnotes(spec)[[1]]$content
stopifnot(grepl("ADSL", fn))

# Check page defaults
pg <- fr_get_page(spec)
stopifnot(pg$orientation == "landscape")
stopifnot(pg$font_size == 9)

# Check visible columns
cols <- fr_get_columns(spec)
visible <- names(cols)[vapply(cols, function(x) {
  vis <- x$visible
  if (is.null(vis)) TRUE else vis
}, logical(1))]
stopifnot("group" %in% setdiff(names(cols), visible))

Style debugging

fr_style_explain() shows the complete resolution chain for a style object, which is useful when debugging overlapping styles:

spec <- tbl_demog |>
  fr_table() |>
  fr_styles(
    fr_row_style(rows = 1, bold = TRUE),
    fr_col_style(cols = "total", bg = "#EBF5FB")
  )
fr_style_explain(spec, row = 1, col = "total")
#> 
#> ── Style explain: row 1, col "total"
#> Content: "135"
#> 2 matching styles:
#> [1] row: bold=TRUE
#> [2] col: bg="#EBF5FB"
#> 
#> Final: bold=TRUE, italic=FALSE, fg=#000000, bg=#EBF5FB, align=left, valign=top,
#> indent=0

Double-programming workflow

For regulatory submissions, the QC programmer can build a parallel spec and compare:

# Programmer 1: creates the table
spec1 <- tbl_demog |>
  fr_table() |>
  fr_titles("Table 14.1.1", "Demographics") |>
  fr_render("output/t_14_1_1_prod.rtf")

# Programmer 2 (QC): independently creates the same table
spec2 <- tbl_demog |>
  fr_table() |>
  fr_titles("Table 14.1.1", "Demographics") |>
  fr_render("output/t_14_1_1_qc.rtf")

# Compare: diff the RTF files or use tools::Rdiff()
# Identical RTF = verified

The immutable pipeline makes double-programming straightforward — same data + same verbs = identical output.

What’s Next

For architecture details, the render pipeline, custom backends, and the sentinel system: vignette("extending-tlframe").

On this page

Developed by Vignesh Thanikachalam.

Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
